insight: Tailwind CSS 4 uses @import 'tailwindcss' and @custom-variant for dark mode instead of tailwind.config.js. Theme tokens are defined in @theme blocks in CSS. The @tailwindcss/vite plugin replaces PostCSS for Vite projects.

jsdom test environment does not provide window.matchMedia. Mock it in test-setup.ts with a minimal implementation that returns matches:false. This is needed for any hook that reads system dark mode preferences.

TanStack Query poll suppression for optimistic updates: use useIsMutating to check if mutations are pending, then set refetchInterval to false when count > 0. This prevents polling from overwriting optimistic cache during in-flight mutations (STPA H1 pattern).

The Prove It epic sessions can leave dolt sql-server and backend processes (tsx watch, node src/index.ts) running after verification, which holds the Dolt embedded lock and blocks all subsequent bd commands in the loop. The loop script hangs silently on bd commands. Fix: Prove It descriptions should explicitly require stopping all servers before closing. Monitor for stale dolt sql-server processes between loop epics.

When using TanStack Table in React, create a single table instance in the parent component (ListView) and pass it as a prop to child rendering components. This avoids duplicating table state management and prevents the table instance from going out of sync between components that need it (e.g., ColumnVisibilityMenu and IssueTable both need the same table ref).

When parsing URL search params for filter/sort state, always validate against whitelists of allowed values. URL params are user-controlled input and can contain SQL injection attempts or prototype pollution strings. Use Set-based validation before casting to domain types.

This monorepo uses pnpm, not npm. Use 'pnpm add <pkg> --filter <workspace>' to install packages, 'pnpm run test' for tests, and 'pnpm exec tsc' for TypeScript checking. Running npm install fails due to workspace:* protocol.

When using dependency relationships in the beads GUI, the DependencyList component must pass the correct (issue_id, depends_on_id) pair from the dependency record to the remove callback, not try to infer IDs from the current issue context. For 'blocks' relationships where depends_on_id equals the current issue, the issue_id is the other issue, not the current one.

In this monorepo, the backend imports from @beads-gui/shared via compiled dist/ output, not source. After changing shared types, you MUST run 'pnpm --filter @beads-gui/shared run build' before the backend will see the updated types.

When killing the backend dev server after Prove It verification, kill the entire process tree in order: first the tsx watch parent, then the node backend process, then the dolt sql-server child. Using pkill -f alone is insufficient because tsx watch respawns dolt via the DoltServerManager auto-restart logic. Check with ps -p <pid> -o ppid,command to find the full tree, then kill parent-first.

useCommandPaletteActions with useSyncExternalStore causes infinite re-render loops when the actions array is unstable (re-created each render). The notify() call in the effect triggers useSyncExternalStore subscribers, which re-render, which re-create the actions array, which fires notify() again. Fix: stabilize action handler references using useRef and pass a stable (useMemo with empty deps) actions array. This pattern applies to any hook that combines useSyncExternalStore + useEffect-based registration.

Badge/indicator components that use Record<EnumType, Config> lookups MUST include a fallback for unknown values (nullish coalescing). Beads issue types, statuses, and dependency types evolve — the GUI will crash on any type not in the hardcoded map. Use: const config = typeConfig[type] ?? { label: type, className: 'fallback-style' };

The infinity loop script hangs silently when bd commands block on the Dolt embedded lock. Between-epic dependency checks (bd list, bd show, bd prime) will wait indefinitely if a dolt sql-server process is holding the lock. The loop does not set timeouts on these bd calls. When monitoring, check for stuck bd processes (ps aux | grep 'bd ') and stale dolt sql-server processes — kill them to unstick the loop. The root cause is Prove It sessions starting the backend server for verification and not cleaning it up.

ca loop generates its infinity-loop.sh and agent_logs in the current working directory's .compound-agent/, which may resolve to .beads/embeddeddolt/<dbname>/.compound-agent/ if the CWD is the Dolt database directory. The Taskfile LOG_DIR variable must auto-detect the active log location since it can be in either the project root or the Dolt DB directory depending on which session generated the script.

When using beads epic dependencies with Prove It verification epics: wire each Prove It to depend on its parent epic (Prove It runs AFTER epic closes), and wire downstream epics to depend on Prove It (not the epic directly). This creates the chain: Epic -> Prove It -> Next Epic. Do NOT use parent-child deps for this — they create the wrong direction (parent waits for child). Use blocks deps: bd dep add prove-it-id epic-id.

Beads tasks cannot depend on epics (bd dep add returns 'tasks can only block other tasks, not epics'). To create cross-type blocking relationships, change the task to type=epic. Milestones have the same restriction — only epic-to-epic deps work for cross-type blocking.

The advisory fleet (architect Phase 2) provides high-value feedback even with a single CLI (claude-only). Key finding patterns: (1) Security advisor catches CLI injection and binding scope, (2) Simplicity advisor identifies over-engineering (WebSocket vs polling, dual component libs, unnecessary state management), (3) Delivery advisor identifies universal-blocker patterns. The 40% surface MVP recommendation was particularly useful for scoping.

For Playwright video recording of web apps: Playwright's bundled ffmpeg only supports webm output (no mp4/h264). Videos at low bitrate (<200kbps) may fail to render on GitHub. Aim for 500+ kbps by adding more sleep() time between actions (longer recording = more frames = better quality). Use capture-screenshots.mjs for reliable visual evidence since PNGs always render correctly on GitHub.

When building Playwright demo recording scripts: (1) Click specific cells, not table rows — clicking a row often hits the checkbox column instead of navigating. Target the title cell with nth(2) or similar. (2) Always validate video output by extracting frames with ffmpeg -ss <timestamp> -frames:v 1 and visually inspecting them before delivering. (3) Use scrollIntoView for detail page sections instead of fixed pixel scroll offsets — content length varies per issue. (4) Test overlay dismissal before relying on Escape — some modal overlays require clicking a close button. (5) Run a diagnostic script first that takes screenshots after every action to identify failures before recording the final video.

When registering Fastify route handlers that need mutable server state (config, managers), pass getter closures () => value rather than the value directly. Values captured at registration time become stale after dynamic reconfiguration (e.g., setup wizard flow). This applies to any route that references config, connection pools, or service managers.

When running E2E tests with pnpm --filter, the backend CWD becomes packages/backend (not the playwright cwd). This causes metadata discovery to walk up and find the root .beads/metadata.json instead of sample-project's. Always set DOLT_DATABASE explicitly in playwright.config.ts env to avoid this.

VS Code theme mapping to app tokens: VS Code has ~500 tokens but a typical app UI needs only ~20 semantic color tokens. The mapping is a creative exercise — extract representative colors (editor background, foreground, accent, status colors) from VS Code theme JSON files and map them to the app's semantic token system (background, foreground, primary, muted, border, info/success/warning/danger, surface).

Vite projects using import.meta.env.DEV need a src/vite-env.d.ts file with /// <reference types="vite/client" /> for TypeScript to recognize import.meta.env. Without this, tsc fails with 'Property env does not exist on type ImportMeta'. The vitest test runner provides its own type augmentation so tests pass without the file — only tsc -b (build) fails.

FOTC prevention boot scripts in index.html cannot import TypeScript modules. Solution: the useTheme hook writes a serialized cache (colorScheme + colors) to a second localStorage key (beads-gui-theme-cache) alongside the theme ID. The boot script reads this cache to apply CSS variables before React loads. The hook treats boot-script DOM state as authoritative on initial mount.

When refactoring a React hook's return type (e.g., useTheme from binary to multi-theme), search for ALL consumers across the codebase: components, views, test mocks, and integration tests. In this project, header.tsx used toggleTheme, graph-view.tsx compared theme === 'dark', and error-recovery.test.tsx mocked the old shape — all needed simultaneous updates.

Integration verification tests in jsdom cannot test module-level initialization (boot script) because vitest reuses module instances across tests. The useSyncExternalStore-based theme hook's currentSnapshot persists across test runs. For boot-script reload behavior, rely on E2E tests (Playwright). For jsdom integration tests, explicitly call setTheme to reset state rather than depending on module re-initialization from localStorage.

For responsive layouts in this React/Tailwind v4 codebase, use the useMediaQuery hook (packages/frontend/src/hooks/use-media-query.ts) with useSyncExternalStore and window.matchMedia. Standard breakpoints: MOBILE_BREAKPOINT=768px (useIsMobile), TABLET_BREAKPOINT=1024px (useIsTablet). Conditional rendering at the component level (if isMobile return null) is preferred over CSS-only responsive classes for major layout changes like sidebar→drawer, table→card-list, side-panel→slide-over.

When adding mobile responsive layouts, the key fixed-width values that block mobile are: app shell min-w constraints, sidebar fixed widths (w-56), kanban column fixed widths (w-[280px] min-w-[280px]), and split-panel fixed widths (w-[420px]). Each needs conditional rendering: sidebar→hamburger drawer, columns→vertical stack, fixed panels→slide-over overlays. Touch targets must be min 44x44px (use min-h-[44px] and h-11 w-11 for icon buttons).

When adding new fields to shared types (like labelColors on IssueListItem/Issue), all test files with mock data need updating. Use perl -i for bulk mechanical updates, but review the results to avoid adding fields to non-matching types (e.g. FilterState, CreateIssueRequest). Pattern: grep for 'vi.mock.*api-client' to find test files that mock the API client.

When adding new API client exports (like fetchLabels, upsertLabel), all test files that mock '@/lib/api-client' via vi.mock must be updated to include the new exports, or vitest will throw 'No X export is defined on the mock' errors.

The beads-gui backend uses a hybrid write pattern: bd CLI for issue CRUD (via runBd), but label_definitions can use direct SQL writes (queryWithRetry) since they are UI metadata, not core issue tracking data managed by the bd CLI.

Board kanban-card.tsx did not get updated when label colors feature was implemented - it still used plain text-muted-foreground text for labels instead of LabelBadge pills. When adding a feature that touches multiple UI surfaces (list, board, detail, filter), verify ALL surfaces are updated, not just the ones explicitly modified in the feature PR.

WCAG AA contrast ratios can be verified with pure TypeScript - no external deps needed. Use the sRGB linearization formula (threshold 0.04045), standard luminance coefficients (0.2126, 0.7152, 0.0722), and (L1+0.05)/(L2+0.05) ratio formula. Test all color palette entries with describe.each for maintainability.

When adding new exports from @xyflow/react (useReactFlow, BaseEdge, getBezierPath, etc.) to production code, ALL test files that mock @xyflow/react must be updated to include the new exports. Search for vi.mock("@xyflow/react") across all test files. This project has mocks in both src/views/ and src/integration/ directories.

ReactFlow MiniMap nodeColor callback requires hex color strings, not CSS classes or CSS variables. When implementing theme-aware minimap colors, the container (backgroundColor, border) can use CSS variables via the style prop, but individual node colors must be hardcoded hex values. This is a ReactFlow API limitation.

@tanstack/react-virtual with jsdom: virtualizer renders 0 rows because jsdom elements have clientHeight=0. Use a VIRTUALIZATION_THRESHOLD (e.g. 100 rows) to conditionally virtualize only large lists. This also matches the UX requirement and makes small-dataset tests pass naturally without mocks.

In the beads-gui backend, the label_definitions table is created by ensureLabelDefinitionsTable() in the onReady hook, which runs on the REPLICA database. After any write triggers a replica sync (destroyPool → stop server → copy primary → restart server → recreatePool), this table is destroyed because the primary database doesn't have it. Fix: (1) call ensureLabelDefinitionsTable AFTER endSync() in the onAfterWrite callback to avoid deadlocking on the sync barrier, and (2) make fetchLabelColors catch ER_NO_SUCH_TABLE (errno 1146) and return empty colors gracefully. This caused the issues API to return 500 after any write, which broke sequential e2e tests.

When debugging Playwright e2e test failures where tests pass individually but fail in sequence, check the backend API status codes between test runs. In this case, the API returned 200 before writes but 500 after writes due to a missing table. The 'No issues found' empty state was rendered because React Query treated the 500 response as a failed fetch, showing the error/empty state. Running curl against the backend API directly (with the same BEADS_DB_PATH and DOLT_DATABASE env vars as playwright.config.ts) was key to identifying the 500 errors.

react-day-picker v9 uses DayPicker component with mode='single', classNames prop for Tailwind styling, and onSelect callback. No CSS import needed when using classNames - just style each element directly with Tailwind utility classes.

For inline table cell editing in TanStack React Table: define small wrapper components (InlineTitleEditor, InlineAssigneeEditor etc.) that manage their own isEditing state. Pass callbacks through buildColumns params following the existing onStatusChange/onPriorityChange pattern. Use e.stopPropagation() on all interactive elements to prevent row click navigation.

useDraft hook pattern for form auto-save: load from localStorage on mount with JSON.parse in try-catch, debounce saves at 500ms, validate parsed data has at least one meaningful field before considering it a valid draft. Use a ref guard (hasRestoredRef) to prevent re-restoration within a single dialog open lifecycle.

When using native HTML <dialog showModal()>, pressing Escape fires a cancel event that closes the dialog independently of React's synthetic event system. stopPropagation() on React's onKeyDown does NOT prevent this. To prevent Escape from closing a dialog when a child picker is handling it, you must handle the onCancel event on the dialog element and call e.preventDefault() when a child combobox has aria-expanded=true.

Label picker Escape key: stopPropagation alone on React synthetic events does NOT prevent the browser from firing a native cancel event on ancestor <dialog> elements. Must also call preventDefault() to suppress the close watcher. The date picker already did this correctly via a native keydown listener.

When implementing query syntax that modifies filter state, new queries should REPLACE all filter dimensions (start from EMPTY_FILTERS) rather than MERGE with existing state. Users expect typing a new query to set exactly those filters, not accumulate with previous ones. Preserve only orthogonal settings like groupBy that aren't part of query syntax.

In beads-gui, the notification system uses a client-side external store pattern (useSyncExternalStore + localStorage) rather than a backend API, since the app is a local issue tracker. Change detection works by polling the issues list and diffing against a stored snapshot. This pattern works well for v1 without requiring backend modifications.

trigger: install_smoke test failed because pnpm build overwrote rewritten imports
insight: pnpm workspace: plain tsc build overwrites post-processed dist files. When a pnpm workspace package has a post-build step (like import rewriting), running pnpm build re-runs tsc and overwrites post-processed files. This creates a TOCTOU race. Fix: add a post-build verification grep that fails if expected transformations are missing, and make build:dist the ONLY way to produce a publishable artifact.

trigger: review found .d.ts files and re-exports were missed by narrow regex
insight: Inlining workspace packages for npm distribution: use content.replaceAll to catch all import forms (ESM import, export-from, require, dynamic import) instead of a narrow regex like /from "@pearl\/shared"/. Also rewrite .d.ts files (pointing to index.d.ts) so TypeScript consumers resolve types. Always add a post-build verification scan that greps for the original specifier and fails the build if found.

trigger: review found source maps referencing non-existent src/ in published tarball
insight: npm pack files field: use globs like dist/**/*.js and dist/**/*.d.ts to exclude source maps (.js.map, .d.ts.map) without excluding type declarations. Using just 'dist' includes everything. This reduced pearl-bdui tarball from 109 to 87 files and removed broken source map references.

trigger: review found hardcoded 3-level directory traversal for workspace detection
insight: Dev-mode detection in installed packages: walk upward in a loop looking for workspace markers instead of hardcoding a specific depth. Use a two-condition check (workspace file AND project-specific marker) to avoid false positives when installed inside another monorepo. Hardcoded depth breaks if dist structure changes.

pattern.good: let dir = startDir; for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) { if (existsSync(resolve(dir, 'pnpm-workspace.yaml')) && existsSync(resolve(dir, 'packages/frontend/package.json'))) return true; dir = resolve(dir, '..'); }

trigger: npm publish returned 404 for 6 consecutive CI runs before identifying Node version and repo URL as root causes
insight: OIDC trusted publishing for npm requires Node 24 (npm >= 11.5.1). Node 22's bundled npm is too old and silently fails with a misleading 404 on PUT. The 404 is not a real 'not found' — it means OIDC auth failed. Also: the package's repository.url on npm must match the GitHub repo exactly (htxryan/pearl, not ryanandrewhenderson/pearl) or OIDC claims won't validate.

trigger: every E2E test that clicked table rows failed in CI due to skeleton race condition
insight: E2E tests clicking table rows must wait for data rows, not skeleton rows. Skeleton rows render immediately with the table but have no click handlers. Use a data-row selector like 'tbody tr:has(input[type=checkbox][aria-label])' — skeleton checkboxes don't have aria-label. This affects both the seededPage fixture and any helper that clicks rows (navigateToFirstIssue).

trigger: publish.yml never ran because release-please used GITHUB_TOKEN to create the release
insight: GitHub Actions workflows triggered by GITHUB_TOKEN don't trigger other workflows. A separate publish.yml triggered by 'release: published' will never fire when release-please creates the release. Solution: chain the publish job directly into the release-please workflow using outputs.release_created as the conditional.

trigger: command palette Ctrl+K failed in CI headless Chrome on Linux
insight: Keyboard shortcuts using 'meta' modifier only match metaKey (Cmd on Mac). On Linux/Windows and in headless CI, Ctrl+K sends ctrlKey, not metaKey. The matchesBinding function must accept either metaKey or ctrlKey when the binding specifies 'meta', so shortcuts work cross-platform.

trigger: P1 review finding: triplicated DDL and race condition
insight: When functions use queryWithRetry (which calls awaitSync()), they deadlock if called during replica sync before endSync(). Fix: add an optional directPool parameter that bypasses queryWithRetry. In pearl, ensureLabelDefinitionsTable now accepts Pool? and server.ts passes createDoltPool() result directly. This creates the table BEFORE endSync() releases the sync barrier, closing the race window where reads could hit a replica missing the table. The DDL is exported as LABEL_DEFINITIONS_DDL constant from labels.ts to avoid schema drift across call sites.

trigger: Setting up Biome from scratch in pearl monorepo
insight: Biome 2.x for pnpm monorepos: use files.includes with explicit path patterns (e.g. 'packages/*/src/**') instead of files.ignore deny-lists. This prevents linting dist/, node_modules/, sample projects, and config files. Set a11y and style rules to 'warn' initially to avoid blocking CI on pre-existing code. Add lint-staged config: biome check --write --no-errors-on-unmatched for staged files.

When auto-formatting a large codebase with Biome for the first time, commit in three stages: (1) tooling config (biome.json, husky, CI), (2) mechanical formatting changes across source files, (3) substantive code changes. This keeps the formatting commit reviewable separately and makes git blame useful.

When extracting types from a component-layer file to break a circular import, the extracted types file inherits the same circular dependency if it imports from the same lib-layer module that imported the original file. Fix: move shared types to the lib layer (where their dependencies already live) and make the component-layer file a re-export shim. In Pearl's case: FilterState moved from components/issue-table/filter-bar-types.ts to lib/query-syntax.ts because FilterState depends on DateRange and StructuralFilter which are defined there.

dependency-cruiser catches circular imports that TypeScript silently handles at runtime. When refactoring files (especially extracting types), always run pnpm lint:deps after the refactor to detect circular dependencies that tsc won't flag. The no-circular rule in .dependency-cruiser.cjs is the enforcement mechanism for P12 (layered governance).

Pearl frontend vitest.config.ts has a pre-existing TS2769 error (Object literal 'test' not in UserConfigExport). This causes pnpm typecheck and pnpm build to exit non-zero for the frontend package. It does NOT affect test execution (pnpm test passes). When verifying changes, use git stash to confirm failures are pre-existing before investigating.

bd init --server creates metadata.json with field names that bd CLI reads correctly (dolt_server_host, dolt_server_port). Manually creating metadata.json with different field names (dolt_host, dolt_port) does NOT work — bd CLI ignores them and falls back to auto-discovery, connecting to a different server. Always use bd init --server to create CLI workspaces for integration tests.

When comparing SQL writer output to bd CLI output in parity tests, normalize: (1) IDs — hash-mode generates different IDs per path, (2) actor fields (owner, created_by, actor) — SQL uses hostname(), CLI uses git user/BEADS_ACTOR, (3) null vs empty string defaults — SQL omits optional columns (NULL defaults), CLI sets them explicitly to empty, (4) event types — SQL emits commented/dependency_added; CLI emits label_added differently. Use PARITY_FIELDS whitelist per table to focus on semantically meaningful columns.

mysql2 connection pools in tests: SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=0 is session-scoped. With a pool, each execute() may use a different connection. To ensure FK checks are disabled for all subsequent queries, get a single connection from the pool (pool.getConnection()) and use it for all operations, then release it.

Exported regex constants with the g flag are stateful (lastIndex persists between .test() calls). Export without g flag; let consumers add g when creating their own instance. Caught by review in Epic 2 attachment protocol.

When implementing collision disambiguators with fixed-width suffix strategies (e.g., replacing last N chars of a ref), always guard against overflow: if the counter exceeds the representable range (e.g., 0xff for 2 hex digits), throw rather than silently produce invalid-length refs.

For library packages with Web Worker modules: (1) v8 ignore directives are appropriate for browser-only Worker branches in unit tests, (2) add the worker entry as a separate package.json exports path, (3) Vite auto-bundles worker URLs from import.meta.url into separate chunks. Verified in Epic 2 with @pearl/shared.

EXIF parser in encoding-pipeline.ts: JPEG segment lengths can be zero or exceed buffer bounds. Always guard with segmentLength < 2 (minimum per JPEG spec) and segmentEnd <= bufferLength to prevent infinite loops on malformed input. Discovered via security reviewer.

jsdom test environment lacks Blob.arrayBuffer() and OffscreenCanvas/createImageBitmap. For Canvas-based pipelines in Vitest+jsdom: (1) polyfill Blob.arrayBuffer in test-setup.ts, (2) stub OffscreenCanvas and createImageBitmap with vi.stubGlobal, (3) mock convertToBlob to return objects with arrayBuffer() method, not real Blobs.

uint8ArrayToBase64: string concatenation in a loop (binary += String.fromCharCode(bytes[i])) is O(n^2) for large inputs. Use chunked approach: split into 8192-byte chunks with subarray, convert each chunk with String.fromCharCode(...chunk), join, then btoa. This maintains compatibility while being O(n).

@fastify/multipart's declare module 'fastify' augmentation pollutes the global FastifyInstance type, breaking ALL route registrations in the project — even with skipLibCheck:true. Static import, dynamic import, and encapsulated plugin registration all fail. The root cause is TypeScript's ambient module augmentation which fires at type-resolution time regardless of import strategy. Fix: use @fastify/busboy directly to parse multipart bodies. Busboy has no Fastify type augmentation.

Node.js path.normalize() resolves '..' segments, so containsTraversal(normalize(p)) always returns false. A path-traversal guard must check the RAW path string BEFORE normalization. Defense-in-depth: use BOTH raw-string '..' check AND normalize()+startsWith() prefix validation. The startsWith check catches the escape, the raw check catches the attempt.

Using process.pid alone as a tmp file suffix causes collisions in concurrent async requests within the same Node.js process. Fix: add randomness (Date.now + Math.random hex) to tmp file names. This matters for any atomicWrite pattern using tmp+rename.

Node.js writeFile supports { flush: true } option which calls fsync before returning. This replaces the manual pattern of open(fd) + writeFile(fd) + fsync(fd) + close(fd) with a single writeFile(path, data, { flush: true }) call. Simpler and equally safe for atomic write patterns.

Path traversal validation must be applied on BOTH read and write paths. In Epic 6, the POST upload had path-traversal guards (normalize + startsWith + containsTraversal), but the GET serve endpoint initially shipped without them. A security review caught that findAttachmentByRef could follow symlinks outside the attachment directory. Fix: after findAttachmentByRef returns a path, validate normalize(filePath).startsWith(normalize(baseDir) + sep). Same pattern applies to orphan sweep's collectAttachmentFiles — validate collected paths stay within baseDir before stat/unlink.

When testing a sweep/scanner that iterates multiple scopes (e.g., 'project' and 'user' storage), mock resolveAttachmentBase must return DIFFERENT paths for each scope. If both scopes return the same directory, files get double-counted. Fix: in test mocks, return scope-specific paths like resolve(root, '.pearl', 'attachments') for project and resolve(root, '.pearl', 'user-attachments') for user.

Content-addressed file serving (GET by ref) needs a recursive file finder because uploads store files in YYYY/MM subdirectories. The finder must search baseDir recursively for files matching '{ref}.*', skip .tmp files (in-progress atomic writes), and validate the resolved path stays within baseDir. Use readdir(baseDir, { recursive: true }) and match on filename prefix, not just name equality, since the extension varies by MIME type.

When writing custom remark plugins for react-markdown that use regex with the g flag (e.g. for matchAll), NEVER use module-level regex constants. Create fresh RegExp instances per call to avoid shared lastIndex state causing pill skipping when multiple markdown renderers invoke the plugin concurrently in the same React render cycle.

In React 19, calling setState or triggering async loads during render (e.g. inside a useContext consumer's render body) violates React's rules and can cause infinite render loops. When a hook needs to trigger loading on first use, move the trigger to useEffect — never fire side effects from inside render. Pattern: split context into getCached (pure reader) + requestLoad (effect trigger).

For remark plugins producing custom inline components in react-markdown v10: use hName/hProperties in the MDAST node data field to map to custom HTML elements, then register the component in react-markdown's components prop. No need for explicit remarkRehypeOptions handlers — hName/hProperties is the bridge. Lift remarkPlugins and components arrays to module-level constants to avoid re-creating the remark processor on every render.

When adding a computed boolean column derived from scanning text fields (like has_attachments scanning description/design/notes/acceptance_criteria), always export the list of scanned fields as a shared constant (ATTACHMENT_HOST_FIELDS) from the shared package. The write-time compute and read-time reconciliation code both need the same field list, and duplication creates a silent divergence risk when new text fields are added.

INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS queries in multi-database environments (like Dolt server mode) must include TABLE_SCHEMA = DATABASE() to avoid matching columns in other databases. Without this filter, ALTER TABLE migrations can be silently skipped when another database has a table with the same name and column.
